messages.content:
Vacation time! How glad it seemed When as a boy I sat and dreamed Above my school books, of the fun That I should claim when toil was done; And, oh, how oft my youthful eye Went wandering with the patch of sky That drifted by the window panes O'er pleasant fields and dusty lanes, Where I would race and romp and shout The very moment school was out.My artful little fingers then Feigned labour with the ink and pen, But heart and mind were far away, Engaged in some glad bit of play The last two weeks dragged slowly by: Time hadn't then learned how to fly. It seemed the clock upon the wall From hour to hour could only crawl, And when the teacher called my name, Unto my cheeks the crimson came, For I could give no answer clear To questions that I didn't hear 'Wool gathering, were you?" oft she said And smiled to see me blushing red. Her voice had roused me from a dream Where I was fishing in a stream, And, if I now recall it right, Just at the time I had a bite.

And now my youngsters dream of play In just the very self-same way, And they complain that time is slow actly the same And that the term will never go.

wetended

same

Their little minds with plans are filled For joyous hours they soon will build, And it is vain for me to say,

That have grown old and wise and gray, That time is swift, and joy is brief;They'll put no faith in such belief. To youthful hearts that long for play Time is a laggard on the way. 'Twas, oh, so slow to me back then Ere I had learned the ways of men!

-Edgar Albert Guest

So let them dream and plan and yearn For days of freedom and the sun, For childhood is a fleeting time Where joy and play should be the prime. And though they may not understand The passing of time's gentle hand, Let them enjoy each moment free And cherish these sweet memories. For soon enough they will be grown And time will speed, as it has shown, So let them revel in this break And all the joy that it can make. Vacation time, oh sweet release, Enjoy it now, and let it cease.
What's name of this poem?
This poem is titled "Vacation time!" by Edgar Albert Guest.
You already knew about it?

Yes, I have access to a large amount of information and can recognize and provide details about various poems, including their title and author. If you have any other questions or need further information, feel free to ask!
Alright so give me short simple summary of each passage for class 8th standard

Sure, here are short and simple summaries of each passage from the poem "Vacation time!" by Edgar Albert Guest for 8th-grade students:

1. The first passage describes the excitement and anticipation a young boy feels for the upcoming vacation. He daydreams and imagines all the fun and play he will have once school is over.

2. In the second passage, the poet talks about how as a child, he would pretend to study while actually daydreaming about the adventures and playtime awaiting him during the vacation. He eagerly awaits the end of school and the start of his holiday.

3. The third passage reflects on how time seemed to move slowly for the poet when he was a young boy waiting for the vacation. He describes how the clock at school seemed to drag, and he eagerly anticipated the freedom and joy that awaited him once school was over.

4. In the fourth passage, the poet compares his own childhood experience of longing for the vacation to that of his own children, who now eagerly await their break from school. He observes how children's perception of time can be different from adults, as they eagerly anticipate and dream of their upcoming vacation.

5. The final passage conveys the poet's advice to let children enjoy their vacation time and cherish the moments of play and joy. He emphasizes the fleeting nature of childhood and the importance of savoring each moment of freedom and fun before they grow up.
5 and 6 paragraph summary

In the fifth paragraph, the poet reflects on his own childhood experiences of eagerly anticipating the vacation and daydreaming about the fun and playtime that awaited him. He recalls how time seemed to move slowly as he waited for the end of the term, and he empathizes with his own children who now eagerly anticipate their break from school. The poet highlights the innocence and excitement of childhood, where time seems to drag as children anxiously await the freedom and joy of the vacation.

In the sixth paragraph, the poet encourages children to revel in their vacation time and cherish the moments of play and freedom. He acknowledges that childhood is a fleeting time and advises them to make the most of their break from school. The poet reminds the readers that as they grow older, time will pass quickly, and the carefree days of play and joy will become cherished memories. He urges children to embrace the present moment and enjoy the simple pleasures of their vacation, as it will soon be over and they will look back on it with nostalgia.

My artful little fingers then Feigned labour with the ink and pen. But heart and mind were far away, Engaged in some glad bit of play

The last two weeks dragged slowly by Time hadn't then learned how to fly.

It seemed the clock upon the wall

From hour to hour could only crawl.

And when the teacher called my name, Unto my cheeks the crimson came, For I could give no answer clear To questions that I didn't hear.

Wool gathering, were you?' oft she said And smiled to see me blushing red.

Her voice had roused me from a dream Where I was fishing in a stream, And, if I now recall it right, Just at the time I had a bite.

And now my youngsters dream of play In just the very self-same way;

And they complain that time is slow And that the term will never go.

same

Their little minds with plans are filled For joyous hours they soon will build, And it is vain for me to say, That have grown old and wise and gray, That time is swift, and joy is brief; GIVE ME SUMMARY OF THESE LINES

In these lines, the speaker reminisces about their childhood days when they would pretend to work with their pen and ink while their mind was actually engrossed in daydreams of play. The speaker recalls how time seemed to drag slowly during the last two weeks of school before the vacation, and the clock on the wall appeared to move at a snail's pace. The speaker describes feeling embarrassed when called upon by the teacher, as they were lost in their thoughts and unable to respond clearly. The teacher would often comment on their distraction, but the speaker was too absorbed in their daydreams to pay attention. 

The speaker compares their own childhood experience to that of their youngsters who also eagerly dream and plan for the joy and playtime of the upcoming vacation. The children complain that time is moving slowly and that the term will never end. The speaker acknowledges their own wisdom and age but notes that the children perceive time differently, believing that it is slow and the vacation will never arrive. The children's minds are filled with plans for joyful activities, and the speaker recognizes the futility of trying to explain that time is swift and joy is fleeting in the eyes of a child.
Short easy simple

These lines talk about how as a child, the speaker would pretend to work while their mind was focused on play. Time seemed to go by slowly as they eagerly awaited the vacation. The speaker would daydream and get lost in their thoughts, even when called upon by the teacher. They compare their own childhood experience to that of their children, who also eagerly anticipate the vacation, believing that time is moving slowly and the break will never come. The children are filled with excitement and plans for the fun they will have, while the speaker reflects on the fleeting nature of joy and time as an adult.

They'll put no faith in such belief. To youthful hearts that long for play Time is a laggard on the way. 'Twas, oh, so slow to me back then Ere I had learned the ways of men!

Give me summary this?

In these lines, the speaker reflects on how children, with their youthful hearts longing for play and excitement, perceive time differently from adults. The speaker acknowledges that children, eagerly anticipating the vacation, do not believe that time moves swiftly. To them, time feels slow and lingering as they wait for the joy and freedom of the break. The speaker recalls that in their own childhood, before they had matured and learned the responsibilities of adulthood, time also seemed to pass slowly as they eagerly awaited the vacation. The lines highlight the contrasting perceptions of time between children and adults, with children feeling time as a laggard as they eagerly await moments of joy and play.

The poet describes a person planning numerous activities for their upcoming vacation with excitement. However, while daydreaming in the classroom about white sharks, the person is suddenly called by the teacher, which fills them with fear. I apologize for any confusion earlier. Here is the completed summary based on the provided text:

The teacher's voice roused him from his dream of being bitten by a white shark in the classroom, causing a sudden wave of fear and embarrassment. The interruption serves as a stark reminder of the contrast between the speaker's imaginative childhood fantasies and the sobering reality of the school environment. This moment captures the whimsical nature of daydreams in childhood and the abrupt awakening to the demands of the classroom setting.
SUMMARY OF POEM VACATION TIME BY EDGAR IN VERY VERY VERY VERY SIMPLE EASY WORDS IN A SHORT WAY ANSWER ME

The poem "Vacation Time" by Edgar Albert Guest talks about how children eagerly wait for their vacations and daydream about fun activities they will do. They feel time goes very slowly before the break. The poet compares his own childhood excitement to that of his children. It shows that children see time differently and eagerly anticipate their playtime.
A little in detail and long

The poem "Vacation Time" by Edgar Albert Guest captures the excitement and anticipation children feel as they eagerly await their vacation. The speaker reminisces about their own childhood, where time seemed to drag on endlessly before the break from school. They would daydream about the various activities and adventures they planned to enjoy during their time off. The poem also compares this youthful exuberance to the similar anticipation seen in the speaker's own children, highlighting the universal experience of eagerly looking forward to vacation time. This contrast in perception of time between children and adults underscores the joy and eagerness children have for the moments of freedom and play that come with the vacation. The poem ultimately celebrates the innocence, excitement, and anticipation that children feel as they eagerly await the fun-filled days ahead during their much-anticipated vacation.

It reflects on how time seems to slow down before the break, as kids eagerly dream about the fun activities they will do. The poem also compares this anticipation to the speaker's own childhood, emphasizing the joy and eagerness children feel as they wait for their holiday.

It emphasizes the joy and eagerness children feel as they anticipate their playtime during the holiday.

In the latter part of the poem "Vacation Time" by Edgar Albert Guest, the speaker reflects on how children, with their youthful hearts yearning for play, perceive time as slow and never-ending. The poet contrasts this with their own experience back in childhood when time seemed to drag before they learned the responsibilities of adulthood. The lines convey that to children eagerly waiting for playtime, time feels sluggish and endless. As an adult looking back, the poet recalls a time when they also felt time moving slowly, before life taught them the swift passage of time. This reflection shows the enduring anticipation and excitement that children hold for their cherished vacation and playtime.

The poem "Vacation Time" by Edgar Albert Guest shows how children eagerly wait for vacations and feel time passes slowly. The children see time as moving very slowly as they long for play and fun. The poet compares this to their own childhood, when time seemed slow before they grew up and learned about life's responsibilities. It highlights how kids eagerly await their playtime, while adults realize how fast time really moves.

Summary of this please in short way

Children eagerly anticipate playtime and complain that time moves slowly before the vacation begins. Their minds are filled with plans for the joyous hours ahead, reflecting the same excitement the speaker felt in their own youth. The speaker, now older, realizes that children perceive time differently, feeling it drags as they eagerly await their fun-filled break. The contrast between youthful anticipation and the swiftness of time in adulthood is noted, with the speaker reminiscing about their own experience of time feeling slow in childhood before they gained maturity.
